chunk_summary: The 'deadly trio' of SaaS marketing channels: SEO/AI search (showing up when prospects are searching), YouTube (searchable videos building trust at scale), and LinkedIn (founder/exec brand with paid amplification). These three work together for high-ACV deals because buying committees need multiple touchpoints across a long research journey — and all three compound over time instead of stopping when ad spend stops.
content: There's one specific marketing strategy that's been driving demos for us every week at my company, Breaking B2B. And I'm yet to find a more powerful strategy for SAS companies, but it's not a single channel. It's a trio of channels working together. SEO and AI search, YouTube, and LinkedIn with both founder brand and paid ads. And if I just joined a B2B SAS company as a CMO or marketing leader, this is exactly what I triple down on. So, in this video, I'm going to walk you through each channel, explain exactly how to execute across all three. I'll explain why they all work so well together, so you can follow along and drive more pipeline and revenue for your SAS company. ... So, here's how the deadly trio works. So, first up, we've got SEO and AO showing up at the top of Google's organic search results and being recommended in AI search when dream clients are needing your solution. The second is YouTube, building searchable videos that build trust at scale. And lastly, we've got LinkedIn, building a founder or exec brand, as well as amplifying those posts with paid ads. And here's why these three work so well together, especially for high ACV, high average contract value solutions. First off, if your SAS sells a solution that's 10K, 20K, 50K, 100K plus as an average contract value, no one wakes up and decides to invest that amount of cash into a piece of software. There's a long old process that goes on behind the scenes. They're not making impulse decisions off the bat. Your dream clients will go almost for a process of having a super expensive problem, maybe trying to fix it in house, maybe getting so frustrated that it doesn't work out, then researching options, evaluating options, and eventually deciding together with their team which solution, which brand, which company makes sense to move forwards with. And as well as this, there may well be a buying committee involved or several stakeholders that are going to be involved in that buying process. And these three channels allow you to hit those dream clients at different points in the buying process, building trust throughout. And unlike classic paid media that stops working the moment you switch off, these three channels all compound over time. The content that you're going to build out can work for you for months or even years to come. You're building assets for your company. You're not renting attention.
questions_on_this_chunk: What is the deadly trio of SaaS marketing channels?
Why do SEO, YouTube, and LinkedIn work well together?
Why is a multi-channel approach important for high-ACV deals?
How do owned media assets compound over time?

chunk_summary: SEO and AI search foundation: despite AI tools only representing ~2% of the search market, the game has changed — you now need to rank in both Google organic and LLMs. The key is focusing on 'money keywords' (competitor alternatives, best X software, industry-specific searches) — low search volume but super high buying intent. Competitor alternative content can rank in days, not months.
content: So, first up, let's start with SEO and AAO because this is going to be building out the foundation. And the crux of it is to show up top of Google organic search and be recommended on AI search and LLMs when dream clients need your solution. Now, this could be when they're comparing options, when they directly need your software or category type now, when they're looking for a specific solution for an industry or vertical that they're within, when they may be frustrated with the incumbent vendor, or they're looking to solve a crisp and specific problem. SEO and AI search should always start from a demand collection perspective. What are my dream clients looking for? Not when they're information gathering, but when they're actually ready to speak to sales, grab a sign up, or grab a demo. And at this stage, you might be thinking, Sam, isn't SEO dead? I mean, didn't AI search and LLM optimization completely take over? Does anyone even use Google search anymore? Well, according to a report from Datas and Spark Toro, Google still gets 373 times more searches compared to Chat GBT, all the AI tools combined. Still make up around 2% of the search market. So SEO isn't dead. The game has simply changed. Now we want to show up in LLMs and AI search as well as classic Google organic results. But the good news is before you panic, we've got around 40 active B2B SAS clients at breaking B2B and solid bottomfunnel pipeline focused SEO carries through to getting visibility on AI search in LLM with a few tweaks here and there. So let's dive into what's working today. So a good way to think about SEO and AI search now, especially if like we were talking about earlier, you're selling solutions that are reasonably high ticket 10K, 20K, 50K, 100K plus annual contracts. often times now it's about getting in the consideration set. ... And like many things with solid SAS marketing, it all starts with the buyer with your dream client and customer research. So in this instance for SEO and AO, what is my dream client looking for when they're in market? So, I recommend following a process called mapping out your money keywords. This is every way a target prospect could search for your solution when they're in buying mode. So, in SAS, some of the common money keywords around this could be best x software, best x tools, best x platforms. It could be competitor alternative, competitor pricing, competitor reviews. It could be best x software for industry or use case or ICP. ... And you can use a tool like Hrefs where you can actually map out the difficulty of these money keywords. The lower the better. But what you'll often find is that these bottomfunnel pipeline driving money keywords will often have relatively low search volume, especially if you're doing competitor alternative, competitor one versus competitor 2 versus you, or competitor pricing or those kind of competition based money keywords. Those ones will often have quite a low search volume but super high buying intent. Folks aren't searching this for fun. They're searching it because they're genuinely in market looking to move away from the incumbent or evaluating options like yours. they have a high chance to drive signups, sales calls or demos. And we've seen this type of competitor content rank as quick as days. I'm not talking months, years. I'm talking days from being published on SAS websites.
questions_on_this_chunk: Is SEO dead now that AI search exists?
What are money keywords in SaaS SEO?
How fast can competitor alternative pages rank?
Why should I target low-volume keywords for SaaS?

chunk_summary: How to build money pages that rank and get cited by AI: research what type of page already ranks (usually listicles for competitor alternatives), structure it as a 'best X alternatives' article. Position your product as #1 backed by customer research and social proof — not marketing fluff. Show product GIFs, feature breakdowns, pros/cons, pricing, and fair comparisons of competitors. Then get featured on external niche sites by offering value exchanges (podcast guesting, reciprocal links, custom Loom outreach).
content: And as a quick example, if I do a Google search for warmly alternatives, I can see our client RB2 mentioned in the Google AI overviews and also the top organic result here. And this is exactly what I was talking about. The crux to this is working out what type of page ranks effectively for the money keyword. So in this case, warmly alternatives. They tend to be what we call a listicalbased article. So we evaluate the seven best warmly alternatives of 2026. And usually these are structured as an article-based listical. You normally have the author, the publish date, you have your H1 with your money keyword. You do a quick intro into what the topic's about, why you should consider alternatives, maybe leverage some intel from platforms like G2 or relevant review sites. So why folks look to switch from the competitor, in this case, why folks tend to get frustrated or switch from warmly. Then you generally do a table of the options. We position ourselves RB to be number one. But we don't just shout about how awesome and how great we are. We leverage real customer insights and feedback. Why folks are choosing us, features they care about, show a screenshot visuals of our product in action, maybe even a GIF of when our product clicks into play for RB2B. ... you have your features, you break down the pricing, you show the pros, where it's a great fit, cons where it's not such a good fit, and then a fair comparison of the other options on the market. ... And the nice thing here is what's being cited is our own list. So you'll see it's actually pulling from our own article. We're really controlling the narrative by building these money pages on our website. ... So once you've controlled the narrative on your own site, a big thing is getting featured in niche relevant sites, getting that brand mention. And one fairly simple way to work out what to do here is look at the websites that keep getting cited for your money prompts, your money keywords. ... And a simple way to do this on chat GBT is you can click the sources tab. You can see all the articles in this case listicles that are pulled from and you can make a process to manually reach out to these folks. You can see who the author is of the content. Perhaps you reach out to them on LinkedIn or relevant channel. You do some kind of pattern interrupt outreach. So maybe you send them a customized Loom. Maybe you offer them a value exchange like maybe you can get them a placement on your article in return for them mentioning you as a top three placement within theirs with your brand mentioned and a nice backlink. or perhaps you offer them some kind of other value exchange, whether that is guesting on your podcast, some kind of interview, or something else that could be of both value to them as well as yourself.
questions_on_this_chunk: How do I structure a competitor alternatives page?
What makes a listicle rank in AI overviews?
How can I get my SaaS mentioned on other sites' listicles?
What is a value exchange for backlinks and brand mentions?

chunk_summary: YouTube strategy for SaaS: use a 'YouTube-first' approach with strong hooks, thumbnails, and scripting. Give away complete playbooks and strategies — so much value that prospects could do it themselves. This builds unrivaled trust because video puts a face to the brand. Stay focused on ideal customers, not vanity views. Embedding YouTube videos on site pages helps SEO and click-through rates.
content: Now, it's time for the second channel of the Deadly Trio. It's YouTube. And to be crystal clear here, I'm not talking about throwing old company webinars up onto the company YouTube and hoping for the best. This is all about crafting searchable videos that share every aspect of your process, playbooks, and more, as well as giving away your strategies and the problems that you're solving for dream clients. But why bother? Well, it's the second largest search engine after Google. Watching someone on video builds another layer of trust that written content really struggles to match. Prospects are able to put a face to the name, to the brand, to the company. They can see you more and more. They understand your thinking and how you approach things. And just like SEO and AI search, it compounds over time. So videos can drive views and inbound leads not just now, but for months and years to come. So here's how you can make YouTube work for your SAS. You've got to go in with a YouTube first approach. That means solid packaging, thumbnails, titles, scripting, and much more. A strong hook to get your dream clients enticed to watch the whole video. A clear story, good pacing, and obvious call to actions on the next step you want folks to take. And you need to stay painfully focused on your dream clients, not trying to get views from anyone and everyone. ... We'd much rather a few hundred views from absolute perfect fit customers with that intent instead of hundreds of thousand views from folks that will never ever become a customer. And in order to make this content actually work, I recommend giving away so much value that your dream client could just watch this and then be able to know how to do it in house. Or of course, the perfect outcome is then they trust you so much, they trust your brand, that you're the brand of choice they're going to go to to deliver for them or use your software. ... Here's a quick bonus. Your YouTube videos can actually help your SEO program, too. So, we'll often embed relevant YouTube videos onto articles, list of cool pages, landing pages, and much more. Not only do they seem to help your organic results, but they can also get pulled up in Google's video section. And sometimes you get a little thumbnail next to your Google organic search result. So, it can improve your click-through rates. It can improve engagement time. ... Plus, YouTube's often picked up in AI search results and LLMs, too.
questions_on_this_chunk: What is a YouTube-first approach for B2B SaaS?
Why should I give away my full playbook on YouTube?
How does YouTube help SEO?
Should SaaS companies chase views or focus on ideal customers?

chunk_summary: LinkedIn organic and paid strategy: the founder or exec posts daily to become the trusted voice in the niche. Use edutainment — memes, skits, video breakdowns — to get attention, then link back to the solution. Build your network systematically with Sales Navigator. Once organic is running, amplify top posts as thought leader ads to two audiences: retargeted website visitors and cold ICP lists. Even $2-3K/month drives booked calls by keeping you top of mind through the long buying cycle.
content: And the last part of the deadly trio, it's LinkedIn organic founder brand or exec brand and paid. So, LinkedIn has two parts to it. Organic posting from your founder or execs on your team onto LinkedIn, as well as paid amplification of those posts, either retargeting website visitor ICPs, as well as going after cold ICP, cold ideal client profile lists. This channel is going to work best if you've got a thought leader on your team, be it your founder, an exec, yourself, that's ideally already got a network full of dream clients or idol client profiles, got several thousand connections or similar. And if not, of course, you can build that up over time. LinkedIn allows you to connect to 100 or so folks each and every week. So, with a tool like Sales Navigator, you can simply search for folks that fit that dream client profile and connect with them over time, building up your network. But the main goal here from organic posting is to become a trusted voice for your ICP in your niche. So when your target prospects have the problem you solve or need your solution or folks are asking about your solution or looking for recommendations, your brand, your SAS company is the one that instantly comes to mind that gets recommended or gets considered or gets chosen. So this is usually posting each day organically to LinkedIn. It could be industry tips. It could be breakdowns. It could be revealing your strategies. It could be memes around your industry. It could be industry news. It could be video skits. It could be podcast clips or so much more. I'm a big fan of edutainment. So, getting attention from ICP first, then linking it back to our solution. So, I tend to do a bunch of memes, videos, skits, as well as detailed breakdowns about some of the clients and companies that we work with, literally leaking the full playbook and strategy, so folks could even copy themselves or trust us so much they come to our brand. And this is all about staying top of mind with your ICPS when they're not in market. ... And then once you, an exec on your team or your founder, has got that organic playbook running. They're posting consistently. They're building their network. They're also, I should add, engaging with other folks in your niche, whether that is dream clients, maybe similar companies, maybe other folks that your ICPs follow and get trusted info from. And once you've got that organic playbook rolling, that's when you want to look to amplify these posts with paid. This is all about taking some of your top organic posts. So, it could be social proof posts, case studies, reviews, or similar. It could be breakdowns. It could be strategies. It could be edutainment ones like memes or skits or videos. And as well as your own posts, it could also be clients or partners that have posted about you and your company and you can sponsor those too. And then you can run them as thought leader ads to two audiences. So firstly, you can retarget your own website visitors, the folks that have already shown interest in your offer. And secondly, you can serve these to a cold list of your ICPs. ... Right now, we spend around 2 to 3K USD per month on the ads. And the main thing here is that a lot of folks in the B2B space, as long as you know your dream clients are hanging out on LinkedIn, it's going to make sense. So, it's been really powerful for us to stay top of mind with folks in the pipeline, retarget those website visitors, stay top of mind, and build trust. And thirdly, to get the attention of those net new cold prospects over time, building trust and brand equity for our company.
questions_on_this_chunk: How do I build a founder brand on LinkedIn for SaaS?
What types of LinkedIn posts work best for B2B?
How should I use LinkedIn thought leader ads?
What budget do I need for LinkedIn ads to work?

chunk_summary: How the flywheel spins: a prospect searches a problem on Google or ChatGPT, finds your content, visits your site, watches a YouTube video, leaves without converting — but flags your brand to their team. They enter your LinkedIn retargeting pool, see founder content and thought leader ads for weeks, return to YouTube for more research, then do a final comparison search where you dominate. After multiple trust-building touchpoints across all three channels, you're the obvious choice and they book a call.
content: And let's wrap up by talking about how the deadly trio works together. So now you understand how each channel works individually, but the real magic happens when they all work together in sync. So here's exactly how the flywheel starts spinning. So someone might search for a crisp and expensive problem you solve. Maybe they're looking directly for the solution itself or maybe evaluating vendors, be it on Google, chat GBT or an AI tool. Your page or content might show up. Your brand might be recommended. Maybe they flick through to one of your site pages. Maybe they then watch a YouTube video. They might then leave your website without converting, but perhaps they flag up your brand to an exec member in Slack or the rest of their team mention it at the next company meeting. They've now been added to your retargeting pool. So over the next few weeks, they're going to be seeing your LinkedIn thought leadership ads. Then they might start following you or your exec or your founder and then consistently see the organic post as well as the sponsored thought leader ads. Perhaps they then head to YouTube and then search for a niche related topic, see one of your videos top, watch the whole thing, and then really believe that your brand is the one that they should trust to solve their problem. And then maybe for final piece of mind after this whole process, they've maybe had a few chats with their exact team. They then do another comparison on Google or Chat GBT of you versus another brand versus the final brand they had on their selection list. They're then clear on your differentiators. They've watched a bunch of your videos. They've seen your founder or execs posting organic on LinkedIn and strongly believe that your offer is the right fit for them. You're the obvious choice, so they sign up to your software, grab a demo, or book a call with sales. And as I say, this deadly trio is especially powerful for high ACV offers. Deals with multiple stakeholders do not close after one touch. You need to keep showing up in the places that your dream clients are getting trusted information from. All throughout the process, from research to consideration to evaluation and beyond. And all three channels really complement each other. SEO and AI search to get you in that consideration set and also at the end capture demand. YouTube to build that extra layer of trust that's unrivaled with video. And LinkedIn to constantly keep you top of mind. And if you've been on LinkedIn for a while, just like I have, you've probably seen the organic reach keeps tanking. The organic reach keeps dropping over time. And that's why this deadly trio becomes more and more important for SAS companies. You're building own media assets that you can actually control. You're not at the mercy of an algorithm. and you're really building out a content engine and flywheel that's going to compound over time.
questions_on_this_chunk: How do SEO, YouTube, and LinkedIn work together as a flywheel?
What does the SaaS buyer journey look like across channels?
Why is retargeting important for high-ACV SaaS deals?
How long does the B2B SaaS buying process typically take?
